questions.q: The flow $\Phi_t$ of a complete vector field $X$ on $M$ satisfies:
questions.explain: The flow is a one-parameter group: $\Phi_{t+s}=\Phi_t\circ\Phi_s$ and $\Phi_0=\mathrm{id}$. Linearity and orthogonality are special properties of particular vector fields.

questions.q: For the linear system $\dot x = -x$ on $\mathbb{R}$ with $x(0)=4$, compute $x(\ln 2)$.
questions.explain: Solution is $x(t)=4e^{-t}$, so $x(\ln 2)=4/2=2$.

questions.q: Match each 1D vector field to its long-time behaviour from $x_0>0$.
questions.explain: $\dot x=-x$: exponential decay to 0. $\dot x=x^2$: $x(t)=x_0/(1-x_0 t)$ blows up at $t=1/x_0$. $\dot x=x$: exponential growth.

hard.q: Which condition fails for $\dot x = x^2$, $x(0)=1$ to be a globally defined flow on $\mathbb{R}$?
choices: Completeness: the solution is defined for all $t\in\mathbb{R}$
hard.hint: The solution blows up at $t=1$.
hard.explain: $x(t)=1/(1-t)$ has a finite-time singularity at $t=1$; the maximal interval is $(-\infty,1)$, so $X(x)=x^2$ is not complete on $\mathbb{R}$.

hard.q: Fix $p$ with $X(p)=0$. To show $\Phi_t(p)=p$ for all $t$, the next step after "the constant curve $\gamma(t)\equiv p$ satisfies $\dot\gamma=0=X(\gamma(t))$" is:
steps: Set $\gamma(t)\equiv p$; verify $\dot\gamma=0=X(\gamma(t))$, so $\gamma$ is an integral curve through $p$.
choices: Differentiate once more to get $\ddot\gamma=0$, hence $\gamma$ is linear.
Invoke uniqueness of integral curves: the flow line through $p$ is unique, so $\Phi_t(p)=\gamma(t)=p$.
Conclude by Hartman–Grobman that $\Phi_t$ is conjugate to its linearization.
Apply Poincaré–Bendixson to the forward orbit.
hard.explain: The ODE theorem gives a unique integral curve through each point; since the constant curve works, it is the flow. Hartman–Grobman and Poincaré–Bendixson are downstream theorems.

hard.q: Let $X(x,y)=(-y,x)$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$. How far from the origin is $\Phi_{\pi/2}(1,0)$?
hard.hint: This is rotation; the flow preserves $x^2+y^2$.
hard.explain: $X=(-y,x)$ is rigid rotation: $\Phi_t(x,y)=(\cos t\,x-\sin t\,y,\sin t\,x+\cos t\,y)$. Distance to origin is preserved; the answer is $1$.

questions.q: A fixed point $p^*$ of $\dot x = X(x)$ is called hyperbolic when:
choices: Every eigenvalue of $DX(p^*)$ has nonzero real part
questions.explain: Hyperbolic = no eigenvalue lies on the imaginary axis. Hartman–Grobman then conjugates the flow to its linearization on a neighbourhood.

questions.q: For $\dot x = \sin x$, the fixed point at $x=0$ has Jacobian $\cos 0$. What is its value?
questions.explain: $X(x)=\sin x$ gives $X'(x)=\cos x$; at $0$, $X'(0)=1>0$, so $0$ is a repelling (unstable) fixed point.

questions.q: For the 2D linear system with matrix $A=\begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 \\ 0 & -2\end{pmatrix}$, the origin is a:
questions.explain: Eigenvalues $1$ and $-2$ have opposite sign, so the origin is a saddle: one expanding direction ($e_1$) and one contracting direction ($e_2$).

hard.q: Which statement about $\dot x=-x^3$ at the origin is correct?
choices: Hartman–Grobman implies the flow is topologically conjugate to $\dot y=0$.
The origin is asymptotically stable even though the linearization is degenerate.
hard.hint: Lyapunov function $V(x)=x^2$ works here; Hartman–Grobman needs hyperbolicity.
hard.explain: $DX(0)=-3\cdot 0^2=0$, so the origin is non-hyperbolic and Hartman–Grobman does NOT apply. But $V(x)=x^2$ has $\dot V=-2x^4\le 0$, so the origin is Lyapunov (even asymptotically) stable.

hard.q: For $A=\begin{pmatrix}0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0\end{pmatrix}$, give one eigenvalue as $a+bi$.
hard.hint: $\det(A-\lambda I)=\lambda^2+1$.
hard.explain: Characteristic polynomial $\lambda^2+1=0$ gives $\lambda=\pm i$; $(0,1)$ or $(0,-1)$ both qualify as answers.

hard.q: Someone 'proves' that every fixed point of $\dot x=X(x)$ is stable. Find the flawed step.
steps: Let $p^*$ be a fixed point, so $X(p^*)=0$.
Since $X(p^*)=0$, the linearized system $\dot y = DX(p^*)y$ has $0$ as a fixed point.
Therefore every trajectory starting near $p^*$ converges to $p^*$.
hard.explain: Step 4 is wrong: nothing in steps 1–3 forces the eigenvalues of $DX(p^*)$ to have negative real parts. Saddles and unstable nodes are fixed points that are NOT attracting.

questions.q: For a 2×2 matrix $A$ with $\operatorname{tr}A=-3,\ \det A=2$, the origin is a:
questions.explain: Discriminant $\operatorname{tr}^2-4\det = 9-8=1>0$, so eigenvalues are real; $\det>0$ + $\operatorname{tr}<0$ means both eigenvalues negative: a stable node.

questions.q: Select ALL systems whose origin is a saddle.
questions.explain: Saddle requires $\det A<0$. (a) $\det=-1$: saddle. (b) $\det=2$: stable node. (c) $\det=3$: node. (d) $\det=-1$: saddle.

questions.q: A nullcline of a 2D system $\dot x=f(x,y),\ \dot y=g(x,y)$ is:
questions.explain: Nullclines are the zero sets of the components; their intersections are fixed points, and they organize the direction field (on the $x$-nullcline, trajectories move vertically).

hard.q: Match $(\operatorname{tr},\det)$ to the portrait type.
hard.hint: Spirals have complex eigenvalues (negative discriminant).
hard.explain: Sign of $\det$ separates saddle from node/spiral. Sign of $\operatorname{tr}$ separates stable/unstable. Discriminant $\operatorname{tr}^2-4\det$ separates node (positive) from spiral (negative).

hard.q: The Van der Pol system $\dot x=y,\ \dot y=\mu(1-x^2)y-x$ with $\mu>0$ has a single fixed point at the origin. What is its type?
hard.hint: Linearize at the origin: $A=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\-1&\mu\end{pmatrix}$.
hard.explain: Jacobian at origin has $\operatorname{tr}=\mu>0$ and $\det=1>0$; for $\mu<2$, $\operatorname{tr}^2-4\det=\mu^2-4<0$ gives complex eigenvalues with positive real part — an unstable spiral. The outward spiral is trapped by a stable limit cycle.

hard.q: Put the steps of sketching a 2D phase portrait in the logical order.
items: Find all fixed points by solving $f=g=0$.
Draw the $x$- and $y$-nullclines and a few sample arrows in each region.
Classify each fixed point via the Jacobian.
Sketch representative trajectories consistent with the local classifications.
Identify any invariant lines or conserved quantities.
hard.hint: Fixed points first; nullclines scaffold the trajectories.
hard.explain: Standard recipe: (1) fixed points, (2) classify them, (3) invariants / symmetries, (4) nullclines, (5) trajectories glued from local pictures.

questions.q: Poincaré–Bendixson asserts that a non-empty compact $\omega$-limit set of a smooth planar flow that contains NO fixed point must be:
questions.explain: In $\mathbb{R}^2$, the trichotomy is: the $\omega$-limit set is a fixed point, a periodic orbit, or a union of fixed points joined by heteroclinic/homoclinic connections. No fixed point leaves only a periodic orbit.

questions.q: Bendixson–Dulac rules out closed orbits in a simply connected region when:
choices: $\operatorname{div}(\phi X)$ has constant sign and is not identically zero on the region, for some smooth $\phi>0$
questions.explain: By Green's theorem, along a closed orbit the integral of $\operatorname{div}(\phi X)$ over the enclosed region would be zero; if the integrand has constant non-zero sign, no such region can exist.

questions.q: For $\dot r = r(1-r^2),\ \dot\theta = 1$ in polar coordinates, the unique stable limit cycle is the circle $r = ?$
questions.explain: $\dot r=0$ at $r=0$ (origin, unstable) and $r=1$ (cycle). Near $r=1$ the radial flow contracts ($\dot r=r(1-r^2)$ changes sign), so $r=1$ is the stable limit cycle.

hard.q: Which system is GUARANTEED to have a periodic orbit by the Poincaré–Bendixson theorem?
choices: $\dot x=-x,\ \dot y=-y$ (sink at origin, no fixed points elsewhere)
A smooth planar system with a compact, forward-invariant annular region $R$ containing no fixed points
Any 3D flow whose vector field is bounded
hard.hint: The theorem is two-dimensional and needs a fixed-point-free trapping region.
hard.explain: A trapping annulus with no fixed point forces the $\omega$-limit set to be a periodic orbit. The theorem fails in 3D, and Hamiltonian systems may have only saddles and centers, not limit cycles.

hard.q: Find the flawed step in this attempt to show $\dot x=y,\ \dot y=-x+y^3$ has no periodic orbit.
steps: By Bendixson's criterion, since $\operatorname{div}X\ge 0$, no closed orbit exists.
Conclude: the system has no periodic orbit in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
hard.explain: Bendixson requires $\operatorname{div}X$ to have STRICTLY one sign on the region (not identically zero). $3y^2$ vanishes on the $x$-axis, so the hypothesis is not satisfied; the argument is invalid.

hard.q: The Van der Pol oscillator has a unique stable limit cycle for all $\mu>0$. For $\mu=0$, what is the amplitude of the (degenerate) circular orbit through $(2,0)$?
hard.hint: $\mu=0$ gives $\ddot x+x=0$: a harmonic oscillator.
hard.explain: At $\mu=0$ the system is $\ddot x+x=0$ (center); every circle is a periodic orbit. Through $(2,0)$ the amplitude is $2$. For $\mu\to 0^+$ the unique limit cycle approaches amplitude $2$.

dyn-iterated-maps.title: Iterated maps and the logistic family

questions.q: For the logistic map $f_r(x)=rx(1-x)$ with $r=2.5$, compute the non-zero fixed point $x^*\in(0,1)$.
A fixed point $x^*$ of a smooth 1D map $f$ is attracting when:
questions.explain: Linearization near $x^*$: $x_{n+1}-x^*\approx f'(x^*)(x_n-x^*)$. Stability needs $|f'(x^*)|<1$.

questions.q: Here are inputs and outputs of an unknown one-parameter logistic map $f_r(x)=rx(1-x)$. Figure out $r$ and compute the requested outputs.

hard.q: The logistic map's non-zero fixed point $x^*=1-1/r$ becomes unstable when $|f_r'(x^*)|=1$. At what $r$?
hard.explain: $f_r'(x^*)=r-2r(1-1/r)=r-2r+2=2-r$. $|2-r|=1$ with $r>0$ gives $r=1$ (emerging) or $r=3$ (period-doubling).

hard.q: After the first period-doubling at $r=3$, the logistic map has a stable 2-cycle $\{p,q\}$. Which equation do $p,q$ satisfy?
hard.hint: A 2-cycle is a pair of points mapped to each other.
hard.explain: A genuine 2-cycle is two distinct points that swap under $f_r$; both are fixed points of $f_r\circ f_r$ but not of $f_r$.

hard.q: For the doubling map $f(x)=2x\bmod 1$, give the 2-periodic orbit $\{p,q\}$ as $p+qi$, with $p<q$.
hard.hint: $f^2(x)=4x\bmod 1$; solve $4p\bmod 1=p$ away from fixed points.
hard.explain: Fixed points of $f^2$: $4x-x\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $x\in[0,1)$ gives $x\in\{0,1/3,2/3\}$. Excluding the fixed point $x=0$, the 2-cycle is $\{1/3,2/3\}$: $f(1/3)=2/3$, $f(2/3)=1/3$.

questions.q: Iterate the logistic map $f_r(x)=rx(1-x)$ at $r=2.5$ from any $x_0\in(0,1)$ for many steps. To what value does $x_n$ converge?
questions.explain: For $1<r<3$ the non-zero fixed point $x^*=1-1/r$ is attracting. At $r=2.5$, $x^*=1-0.4=0.6$.

questions.q: At $r=3.2$ (just past the first period-doubling bifurcation at $r=3$), what is the period of the eventual attracting orbit of the logistic map?
questions.explain: The first period-doubling happens at $r=3$, and the second (to a 4-cycle) at $r_2\approx 3.449$. Between them — including $r=3.2$ — the attractor is a stable 2-cycle.

questions.q: Why do successive period-doubling bifurcations $r_n$ pile up so quickly toward $r_\infty\approx 3.5699$ — accelerating in $r$ but not in $n$?
choices: Each iterate of the logistic map runs twice as fast as the previous one.
The intervals $r_n-r_{n-1}$ shrink geometrically by the Feigenbaum ratio $\delta\approx 4.6692$, so they form a convergent geometric series in $r$ even though $n$ grows linearly.
The Lyapunov exponent diverges to $+\infty$ as $n$ increases.
The orbit becomes chaotic immediately after the first doubling.
questions.explain: Feigenbaum's universal scaling: $(r_{n-1}-r_{n-2})/(r_n-r_{n-1})\to\delta\approx 4.6692$. So gaps between successive bifurcations contract by a constant factor $\approx 1/\delta$ each step — geometric convergence in $r$-space, even though $n$ marches up linearly. That is why infinitely many doublings fit into the finite interval $[3, r_\infty]$.

questions.q: The normal form $\dot x = \mu - x^2$ undergoes a:
questions.explain: For $\mu>0$ two fixed points at $\pm\sqrt\mu$; at $\mu=0$ they collide; for $\mu<0$ no fixed points. That is a saddle-node.

questions.q: Match each normal form to its bifurcation name.
questions.explain: Saddle-node creates/destroys a pair of fixed points; transcritical exchanges stability between two fixed points; pitchfork bifurcates one fixed point into three (with a cubic symmetry); Hopf births a limit cycle from a spiral.

questions.q: The first period-doubling of the logistic map happens at $r=3$. Which statement is correct about the 2-cycle born there?
choices: It is born stable and replaces the now-unstable fixed point.
It exists only at the single value $r=3$.
It is a continuous limit cycle.
questions.explain: At $r=3$ the fixed point loses stability ($f'(x^*)=-1$); a stable 2-cycle emerges via period-doubling and persists for $r>3$ (until $r_2\approx 3.449$ where it period-doubles again).

hard.q: Select ALL true statements about the supercritical pitchfork $\dot x=\mu x-x^3$.
choices: For $\mu<0$, the only fixed point is $x=0$, which is stable.
For $\mu>0$, $x=0$ is unstable and two new stable fixed points $\pm\sqrt\mu$ appear.
The bifurcation is NOT generic: it requires an $x\mapsto -x$ symmetry to stay robust under perturbation.
hard.hint: All four statements are standard textbook facts about the supercritical pitchfork.
hard.explain: Each statement is the classical lore: symmetry is essential (break it and you get a saddle-node plus a transcritical).

hard.q: A supercritical Hopf bifurcation at $\mu=0$ produces, for $\mu$ slightly positive:
choices: a pair of new fixed points
a small-amplitude stable limit cycle of amplitude $\sim\sqrt\mu$
hard.hint: The normal form is $\dot r=\mu r-r^3$.
hard.explain: Solving $\dot r=0$ gives $r=\sqrt\mu$; a stable limit cycle of amplitude $\sqrt\mu$ emerges as the spiral loses stability.

hard.q: Feigenbaum's constant $\delta$, the universal ratio $\lim_{n\to\infty}(r_{n-1}-r_{n-2})/(r_n-r_{n-1})$ for period-doubling cascades, equals (to 3 decimal places):
hard.hint: Famous constant; starts $4.669\ldots$
hard.explain: Feigenbaum's first constant is $\delta\approx 4.6692016\ldots$, universal across all smooth unimodal maps with quadratic maximum.

questions.q: Compute the Lyapunov exponent of the doubling map $f(x)=2x\bmod 1$ on $[0,1)$.
questions.explain: $f'(x)=2$ almost everywhere, so $\lambda=\int \log|f'(x)|\,dx=\log 2\approx 0.6931$.

questions.q: Sensitive dependence on initial conditions means:
choices: there exists $\delta>0$ such that for every $x$ and every neighbourhood $U\ni x$, some $y\in U$ and $n\ge 0$ give $|f^n(x)-f^n(y)|\ge\delta$
orbits converge to a fixed point
questions.explain: SDIC: arbitrarily close points eventually separate by at least a fixed amount $\delta$ under iteration.

questions.q: Devaney's definition of chaos requires three conditions. Order them from easiest to verify to hardest.
questions.explain: In Devaney's original list (easier → harder): (i) dense periodic points, (ii) transitivity, (iii) SDIC. Banks–Brooks–Cairns–Davis–Stacey later showed (iii) is implied by (i)+(ii) on infinite metric spaces.

hard.q: For the fully-chaotic logistic map $f_4(x)=4x(1-x)$, the invariant density is $\rho(x)=\tfrac{1}{\pi\sqrt{x(1-x)}}$. The Lyapunov exponent is $\int_0^1 \log|f_4'(x)|\,\rho(x)\,dx$. Evaluate.
hard.hint: Conjugate to the tent map via $x=\sin^2(\pi y/2)$; both give $\log 2$.
hard.explain: $f_4$ is semiconjugate to the doubling (or tent) map; the conjugacy preserves Lyapunov exponents, so $\lambda=\log 2$.

hard.q: For the Lorenz system with classical parameters $(\sigma,\beta,\rho)=(10,8/3,28)$, how many Lyapunov exponents are there and what are their signs?
hard.hint: Number of exponents equals the dimension; flows always have a zero exponent along the flow direction.
hard.explain: A 3D flow has three Lyapunov exponents. One is always zero (flow direction, for non-fixed points). For Lorenz with classical parameters, they are approximately $+0.906, 0, -14.57$ — a signature of chaotic attractors in 3D dissipative flows.

hard.q: To compute the Lyapunov exponent of the tent map $T(x)=1-|1-2x|$ on $[0,1]$: the next step after "$|T'(x)|=2$ almost everywhere in $[0,1]$" is:
choices: Apply Birkhoff's ergodic theorem with Lebesgue-invariant measure: $\lambda=\int_0^1 \log 2\,dx=\log 2$.
Use Hartman–Grobman to linearize the map at each point.
hard.explain: Lebesgue measure is invariant and ergodic for the tent map; Birkhoff converts the time average into the space average, giving $\lambda=\log 2$. (The naive 'every orbit' version of (a) is almost-everywhere but not pointwise-everywhere true.)

questions.q: Liouville's theorem states that the flow of $\dot x=X(x)$ preserves phase-space volume if and only if:
questions.explain: $\tfrac{d}{dt}\mathrm{vol}(\Phi_t(U))=\int_U \operatorname{div}X\,d\mathrm{vol}$, so the flow preserves volume iff $\operatorname{div}X\equiv 0$.

questions.q: For the Lorenz system $\dot x=\sigma(y-x),\ \dot y=\rho x-y-xz,\ \dot z=xy-\beta z$, compute $\operatorname{div}X$ with $\sigma=10,\beta=8/3$.
questions.explain: $\operatorname{div}X = -\sigma - 1 - \beta = -10 - 1 - 8/3 = -41/3 \approx -13.67$. Strictly negative, so Lorenz is dissipative.

questions.q: Select ALL systems that are conservative (volume preserving).
choices: Hamilton's equations $\dot q=\partial H/\partial p,\ \dot p=-\partial H/\partial q$
questions.explain: (a) Hamiltonian flow: $\operatorname{div}X=\partial^2 H/\partial q\partial p - \partial^2 H/\partial p\partial q=0$. (b) $\operatorname{div}=-1<0$: dissipative. (c) Pendulum is Hamiltonian with $H=y^2/2-\cos x$. (d) Damped oscillator: $\operatorname{div}=-\mu<0$.

hard.q: A 3D dissipative flow with $\operatorname{div}X<0$ everywhere CANNOT have:
choices: a phase-space-filling invariant torus of positive 3-volume
hard.hint: What happens to a 3-volume under a contracting flow as $t\to\infty$?
hard.explain: Volume contracts to zero, so no 3D invariant set of positive volume survives; attractors must have Lebesgue measure zero. They can still be strange (Lorenz), and limit cycles and chaos are both compatible with dissipation.

hard.q: To prove a Hamiltonian vector field $X_H$ satisfies $\operatorname{div}X_H=0$ on $\mathbb{R}^{2n}$, the next step after "write $X_H=(\partial H/\partial p_1,\ldots,\partial H/\partial p_n,-\partial H/\partial q_1,\ldots,-\partial H/\partial q_n)$" is:
steps: Write $X_H=(\partial H/\partial p_1,\ldots,\partial H/\partial p_n,-\partial H/\partial q_1,\ldots,-\partial H/\partial q_n)$.
choices: Apply Stokes to the boundary of the phase space.
Compute $\operatorname{div}X_H=\sum_i \partial^2 H/\partial q_i\partial p_i - \sum_i \partial^2 H/\partial p_i\partial q_i=0$ by equality of mixed partials.
hard.explain: Compute $\operatorname{div}X_H$ directly: the two sums cancel by Clairaut's theorem. This is Liouville's theorem for Hamiltonian systems.

hard.q: Under a flow with constant $\operatorname{div}X=-0.5$, a phase-space ball of volume $1$ shrinks to what volume after $t=10$ seconds?
hard.hint: Volume evolves as $\mathrm{vol}(t)=\mathrm{vol}(0)e^{\int_0^t \operatorname{div}X\,ds}$.
hard.explain: With constant divergence $c=-0.5$, $\mathrm{vol}(t)=e^{ct}=e^{-5}\approx 0.00674$.

questions.q: A Poincaré section $\Sigma$ for a flow $\Phi_t$ is:
choices: a fixed point of the flow
a codimension-1 submanifold transverse to the flow, used to define a first-return map
questions.explain: $\Sigma$ is locally transversal to $X$; the first-return time $\tau(x)$ is well-defined nearby, and $P(x)=\Phi_{\tau(x)}(x)$ is the return map.

questions.q: A periodic orbit of the flow corresponds, under the Poincaré map, to:
choices: nothing: periodic orbits are invisible to $P$
questions.explain: A closed orbit crossing $\Sigma$ once per period returns to the same point: $P(x^*)=x^*$. Its stability as a cycle = stability of $x^*$ as a fixed point of $P$.

questions.q: For the rigid rotation $\dot r=0,\ \dot\theta=1$ with section $\Sigma=\{\theta=0\}$, the first-return map $P\colon \Sigma\to\Sigma$ has return time equal to:
questions.explain: Starting at $\theta=0$, the flow returns to $\theta=0$ after $\Delta t=2\pi$, so $\tau\equiv 2\pi$.

hard.q: Why does the Poincaré section trick REDUCE the dimension of the problem?
choices: Because $\Sigma$ is $(n-1)$-dimensional when the flow is in $n$-dimensional space, turning an $n$-D flow into an $(n-1)$-D discrete map.
Because the flow becomes linear on $\Sigma$.
hard.explain: A 3D flow restricted to a 2D section is a 2D discrete map; a 2D flow on a 1D section is a 1D map. Each reduction lets you apply lower-dimensional techniques (e.g. 1D maps for analyzing 2D limit cycles).

hard.q: Order the steps in constructing the Poincaré map for a limit cycle.
items: Pick a point $x^*$ on the closed orbit.
Take a small disk $\Sigma\ni x^*$ transverse to the flow.
Solve $\Phi_{\tau(x)}(x)\in\Sigma$ for a return time $\tau(x)$ near $x^*$.
Compute the Floquet multipliers as eigenvalues of $dP(x^*)$.
hard.hint: Geometric construction, then analytic definition, then linearization.
hard.explain: Standard recipe: pick the cycle and a section, produce the return time, define the map, linearize for stability.

hard.q: To show the Poincaré map $P$ is smooth near a fixed point $x^*$, the next step after "the return time $\tau(x)$ is defined by $\Phi_{\tau(x)}(x)\in\Sigma$" is:
steps: The return time $\tau(x)$ is defined by the condition $\Phi_{\tau(x)}(x)\in\Sigma$.
choices: Apply the Inverse Function Theorem to $(x,t)\mapsto\Phi_t(x)$ directly.
Apply the Implicit Function Theorem to the defining equation, using that $X(x^*)$ is transverse to $\Sigma$, to obtain a smooth $\tau(x)$; then $P(x)=\Phi_{\tau(x)}(x)$ is smooth as a composition.
Use the Stone–Weierstrass theorem to approximate $P$ by polynomials.
Invoke Banach's fixed point theorem on $P$.
hard.explain: Transversality $X(x^*)\notin T_{x^*}\Sigma$ is exactly the non-degeneracy needed for IFT to give $\tau(x)$ smoothly; then smoothness of $P$ follows from smoothness of $\Phi$.

questions.q: The doubling map $f(x)=2x\bmod 1$ is topologically conjugate to:
questions.explain: Write $x$ in binary $x=0.b_1b_2b_3\ldots$; then $f(x)=0.b_2b_3\ldots$, i.e. the left-shift on binary sequences.

questions.q: Match the symbolic feature to its dynamical meaning (for the doubling map via binary expansion).
questions.explain: Topological conjugacy transports each property: eventual periodicity, periodicity, and density are all preserved by the coding.

questions.q: Count the number of 2-periodic points of the full 2-shift $\sigma$ on $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ (including fixed points).
questions.explain: Fixed points of $\sigma^2$ correspond to length-2 binary words: $00,01,10,11$ — four sequences in total (including the two fixed points $\overline{0}$ and $\overline{1}$).

hard.q: Why does the itinerary map for the logistic map $f_4(x)=4x(1-x)$ fail to be a homeomorphism onto $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$?
choices: Because the two intervals $I_0,I_1$ meet at the critical point $1/2$, so some points have two itineraries (like dyadic rationals having two binary expansions).
hard.hint: The critical point $x=1/2$ sits on the boundary between $I_0=[0,1/2]$ and $I_1=[1/2,1]$.
hard.explain: Ambiguity at the critical point gives a finite-to-one map rather than a homeomorphism; after identifying pairs like $0\overline{1}=1\overline{0}$ (countably many points), it becomes a semiconjugacy.

hard.q: How many period-$n$ points does the full 2-shift have? For $n=5$, give the count.
hard.hint: Period-$n$ here means fixed under $\sigma^n$ (including lower periods).
hard.explain: Fixed points of $\sigma^n$ correspond to length-$n$ binary words: $2^n$. For $n=5$, $2^5=32$.

hard.q: Select ALL properties that the full 2-shift $\sigma\colon \{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}\to\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ has.
hard.hint: Three of these are Devaney-chaos; the fourth is false for any infinite system with dense periodic + transitive points.
hard.explain: The 2-shift is a model example of Devaney chaos: transitive (a sequence containing every word generates a dense orbit), periodic points are dense (periodic binary strings), SDIC (flip the $n$-th digit to separate orbits after $n$ shifts). Not every orbit is periodic — most are aperiodic.

questions.q: A measure-preserving transformation $T$ of $(X,\mu)$ is ergodic when:
choices: every measurable set $A$ with $T^{-1}A=A$ has $\mu(A)=0$ or $\mu(A)=1$
questions.explain: Ergodicity = the only invariant sets (up to measure zero) are the trivial ones.

questions.q: Birkhoff's ergodic theorem says that for an ergodic $T$ and $f\in L^1$:
questions.explain: Time average = space average almost everywhere, when $T$ is ergodic. This is why statistical mechanics works.

questions.q: Select ALL transformations of $[0,1)$ that are ergodic with respect to Lebesgue measure.
questions.explain: Irrational rotations are uniquely ergodic (Weyl). The doubling map is ergodic (even mixing). Rational rotations split $[0,1)$ into finitely many cycles — not ergodic. The identity preserves every set — far from ergodic.

hard.q: By Birkhoff applied to $f(x)=x$ under $T(x)=2x\bmod 1$, $\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k<N}T^k(x)\to a$ for a.e. $x$. Give $a$ as $a+bi$.
hard.hint: $T$ preserves Lebesgue; $\int_0^1 x\,dx=?$
hard.explain: The doubling map is ergodic w.r.t. Lebesgue, so time averages equal the space average $\int_0^1 x\,dx=1/2$.

hard.q: A Bernoulli shift on $\{0,1\}^{\mathbb{N}}$ with $(p,1-p)$ measure is:
hard.hint: Bernoulli measures are mixing (hence ergodic) for every $p\in(0,1)$.
hard.explain: Independence of cylinders under the shift gives mixing: $\mu(A\cap T^{-n}B)\to \mu(A)\mu(B)$. Mixing implies ergodicity for all $p\in(0,1)$.

hard.q: By Birkhoff, for almost every $x\in[0,1)$, the asymptotic frequency of digit $1$ in the binary expansion of $x$ equals what number?
hard.hint: Apply Birkhoff to $\mathbf{1}_{[1/2,1)}$ under the doubling map.
hard.explain: This is the Borel normal-numbers theorem. The indicator of $\{x\in[0,1):b_1=1\}=[1/2,1)$ has $\int=1/2$; ergodicity of $T(x)=2x\bmod 1$ delivers the claim for a.e. $x$.

questions.q: A strange attractor is invariant, attracting a positive-volume basin, AND:
choices: has sensitive dependence on initial conditions (and typically fractal dimension)
questions.explain: 'Strange' = chaotic dynamics on the attractor + fractal/non-integer dimension. Regular attractors (fixed points, limit cycles, tori) are NOT strange.

questions.q: The Lorenz attractor uses the classical parameters:
questions.explain: $(10,8/3,28)$ are Lorenz's original 1963 values, where the butterfly-wing attractor was first discovered.

questions.q: Estimate of the box-counting dimension of the Lorenz attractor (classical parameters), to 2 decimals.
questions.explain: Numerical estimates give $d\approx 2.05$–$2.07$; it is just above $2$, consistent with an attractor that is almost but not quite a surface.

hard.q: Why must the Lorenz attractor have zero Lebesgue 3-volume despite being an attractor?
choices: Because it is a fixed point.
Because $\operatorname{div}X=-\sigma-1-\beta<0$: the flow contracts volume, so any attractor has zero 3-volume.
hard.hint: Recall the constant divergence of the Lorenz vector field.
hard.explain: Strictly negative divergence contracts 3-volumes exponentially; an attractor, being the intersection of shrinking forward images, must have zero Lebesgue measure.

hard.q: Select ALL true statements about the Lorenz system at classical parameters.
choices: It has three fixed points: the origin and two symmetric non-trivial points.
The non-trivial fixed points are stable.
It exhibits sensitive dependence on initial conditions on the attractor.
hard.hint: At $\rho=28$, the non-trivial fixed points are UNSTABLE (past the Hopf bifurcation at $\rho\approx 24.74$).
hard.explain: Lorenz has 3 fixed points for $\rho>1$; at $\rho=28$ the origin is a saddle, the two symmetric points have already become unstable spirals (subcritical Hopf at $\rho\approx 24.74$), and the attractor exhibits SDIC.

hard.q: Match the attractor to its best description.
hard.explain: The four standard asymptotic behaviours of dissipative flows: equilibrium, periodic, quasi-periodic, chaotic. Dimension climbs from 0 to a fractal value.
